perturbation.original_text: (8) This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Virginia, without regard for Virginia's choice of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Virginia, with regard for Virginia's choice of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that Virginia's choice of law rules will be considered, this creates potential for a conflict because these rules might dictate that another state's laws apply, potentially weakening protections offered under Virginia law. This is a subtle but potentially impactful shift that could negatively impact the disclosing party depending on the specific issue and the alternate jurisdiction's laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause excludes choice of law rules. The modification includes them, which may cause the contract to be interpreted under a different jurisdiction other than Virginia. This could weaken consumer protection rights. VA Code § 1-301 addresses the principles the courts apply to determine which state's laws should govern the contract, which could alter the rights the parties have under it.

Boundaries of the Commonwealth 6/23/2025 § 1-301 . Extent of territory of the Commonwealth after the Constitution of 1776. The authorities in determining the extent of the territory of the Commonwealth after the adoption of the Constitution of 1776 shall consist of: 1. The charter of April 10, 1606, granted by James the First, in the fourth year of his reign, that authorized the first plantation at any place upon the coast of the Commonwealth between the thirty-fourth and forty-first degrees of north latitude; and granted the territory from the seat of the plantation (which under this charter was begun at Jamestown), for 50 miles along the coast towards the west and southwest, as the coast lay, and for 50 miles along the coast, towards the east and northeast, or towards the north, as the coast lay, together with all the islands within 100 miles directly over against the seacoast, and all the territory from the same 50 miles every way on the seacoast, directly into the mainland for the space of 100 miles. 2. The second charter of James, dated May 23, 1609, in the seventh year of his reign, that granted all the territory from the point of land called Cape or
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a significant legal contradiction. The original text specifies that Virginia law governs the agreement without regard to Virginia's choice of law rules. The changed text states Virginia's choice of law rules will be considered. The Law Explanation states that this modification could cause the contract to be interpreted under a different jurisdiction, potentially weakening consumer protection rights. VA Code § 1-301, as noted in the Law Explanation, addresses the principles courts apply to determine which state's laws should govern the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 does contain a part of VA Code § 1-301 which relates to the extent of territory of the Commonwealth, it doesn't directly address choice of law in contracts. However, the explanation combined with the explicit alteration from 'without regard' to 'with regard' creates a clear pathway where the contract could be subject to laws other than Virginia's which introduces a conflict as it undermines the initial agreement on which law governs the contract. The perturbation explanation indicates a potential conflict and weakening of protections under Virginia law.

perturbation.original_text: (4) ARIN covenants and agrees that it will take reasonable care to prevent the disclosure to any person or pe rsons outside its organization or to any unauthorized person or persons specifically identified by Applicant in writing, any and all Proprietary Information which is received from the Applicant under this Non-Disclosure Ag reement and which has been protected in accordance with paragraphs 2 and 3 hereof; provided, however, that ARIN shall not be liable for disclosure of such information if any or all of such information: A. Was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed, or B. Becomes part of the public domain without breach of this Agreement, or C. Is disclosed with the written approval of the Applicant, or D. Is disclosed after three years from ARIN's receipt of the information, or E. Was indepe ndently developed by ARIN, or F. Is or was disclosed by the Applicant to a third party without restriction, or G. Is disclosed pursuant to the provisions of a court order.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) ARIN covenants and agrees that it will take reasonable care to prevent the disclosure to any person or pe rsons outside its organization or to any unauthorized person or persons specifically identified by Applicant in writing, any and all Proprietary Information which is received from the Applicant under this Non-Disclosure Ag reement and which has been protected in accordance with paragraphs 2 and 3 hereof; provided, however, that ARIN shall not be liable for disclosure of such information if any or all of such information: A. Was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed, or B. Becomes part of the public domain without breach of this Agreement, or C. Is disclosed with the written approval of the Applicant, or D. Is disclosed after three years from ARIN's receipt of the information, or E. Was indepe ndently developed by ARIN, or F. Is or was disclosed by the Applicant to a third party without restriction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing exception G, which states that ARIN is not liable for disclosure pursuant to the provisions of a court order, implies that ARIN could be held liable even if legally compelled to disclose information by a court. This omission creates a contradiction because ARIN cannot be held liable if forced to disclose proprietary information pursuant to a court order.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1782 outlines the process and circumstances under which U.S. courts can order a person or entity to provide evidence or information for use in foreign legal proceedings.  By removing the exception for court-ordered disclosures, the modified NDA attempts to contradict the legal obligation to comply with such orders.  ARIN would be compelled by law to comply with a valid court order for disclosure, but the modified NDA suggests they would still be liable for doing so, which is a legal contradiction.

Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals (a) The district court of the district in which a person resides or is found may order him to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing for use in a proceeding in a foreign or internat
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1782 - Assistance to foreign and international tribunals and to litigants before such tribunals U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district court of the district in which a person resides or is found may order him to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing for use in a proceeding in a foreign or international tribunal, including criminal investigations conducted before formal accusation. The order may be made pursuant to a letter rogatory issued, or request made, by a foreign or international tribunal or upon the application of any interested person and may direct that the testimony or statement be given, or the document or other thing be produced, before a person appointed by the court. By virtue of his appointment, the person appointed has power to administer any necessary oath and take the testimony or statement. The order may prescribe the practice and procedure, which may be in whole or part the practice and procedure of the foreign country or the international tribunal, for taking the testimony or statement or producing the document or other thing. To the extent that the order does not prescribe otherwise, the testimony or statement shall be taken, and the document or other thing produced, in accordance with the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. A person may not be compelled to give his testimony or statement or to produce a document or other thing in violation of any legally applicable privilege. (b) This chapter does not preclude a person within the United States from voluntarily giving his testimony or statement, or producing a document or other thing, for use in a proceeding in a foreign or international tribunal before any person and in any manner acceptable to him. (June
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes exception G, which previously stated ARIN would not be liable for disclosure of proprietary information if compelled by a court order. This omission contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1782, as explained in the Law Explanation. Both scraped law snippets confirm that district courts can order a person to produce documents for use in foreign legal proceedings. By removing the exception, the modified contract implies ARIN could be liable even when legally obligated to disclose information, creating a direct conflict with the court's power to compel disclosure under 28 U.S.C. § 1782. The modified NDA attempts to contradict the legal obligation to comply with such orders, creating a legal contradiction, because ARIN cannot be held liable if forced to disclose proprietary information pursuant to a court order.

perturbation.original_text: (5) The standa rd of care for protecting Proprietary Information imposed on ARIN wi ll be th at degree of care ARIN uses to prevent disclosure, publication or dissemination of its own Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) The standard of care for protecting Proprietary Information imposed on ARIN will be reasonable care.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the standard of care with "reasonable care" reduces the accountability of ARIN because they could argue that their actions were reasonable even if their internal standards are lax. This weakens the protection of proprietary information. This introduces a contradiction because many states require a heightened duty of care for protecting sensitive business information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Va. Code § 59.1-336
perturbation.law_explanation: The Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act (VUTSA) implies a higher standard of care than just 'reasonable care' for protecting trade secrets. By only requiring 'reasonable care,' the modified contract provides less protection than VUTSA mandates for information that qualifies as a trade secret, potentially exposing the applicant to harm and contradicting the expected level of protection under Virginia law. This weakens the applicant's rights in case of misappropriation.

Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-336 . Short title and definitions. As used in this chapter, which may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, unless the context requires otherwise: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, use of a computer or computer network without authority, breach of a duty or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. "Misappropriation" means: 1. Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or 2. Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who a. Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or b. At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (1) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (2) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; (3) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (4) Acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural pe
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a specific standard of care ('that degree of care ARIN uses to prevent disclosure...of its own Proprietary Information') with a more general 'reasonable care'. This weakens the protection of proprietary information. The law explanation states that VUTSA implies a higher standard of care than 'reasonable care'. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation', highlighting actions such as unauthorized acquisition or disclosure of trade secrets. By reducing the standard of care, the contract potentially contradicts the level of protection expected under VUTSA for trade secrets, making it easier for ARIN to argue that its actions were 'reasonable' even if they led to misappropriation as defined in the law.

perturbation.original_text: (10) Under no circumstances, including negligence, shall ARIN be liable for any indirect, incidental, consequential, punitive or special damages for any breach of this ag reement, even if ARIN has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.changed_text: (10) Under no circumstances shall ARIN be liable for any indirect, incidental, consequential, punitive or special damages for any breach of this agreement, even if ARIN has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase "including negligence" from the limitation of liability clause attempts to shield ARIN from liability even in cases where their negligence causes damages. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is present in Virginia contracts. It creates a contradiction where ARIN is exempt from gross negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Virginia law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Ward's Equip., Inc. v. New Holland N. Am., Inc., 254 Va. 379, 385, 493 S.E.2d 516, 520 (1997)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Virginia law, every contract contains an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. By removing the phrase 'including negligence,' the clause now implies that ARIN is not liable even for damages resulting from its own negligent actions. This arguably violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as it allows ARIN to benefit from its own wrongdoing at the expense of the Applicant. Ward's Equip., Inc. v. New Holland N. Am., Inc. illustrates the duty of good faith in Virginia Contract Law. This creates an imbalance of power and could be viewed as unconscionable, particularly if the negligence leads to significant damages for the Applicant.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of limitations | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of limitations A statute of limitations is any law that bars claims after a certain period of time passes after an injury. The period of time varies depending on the jurisdiction and the type of claim. There are statutes of limitations for both civil and criminal causes of action . They may begin to run from the date of the injury, the date it was discovered, or the date on which it would have been discovered with reasonable efforts. Many statutes of limitations are actual legislative statutes , while others may come from judicial common law . Compare: s tatute of repose . [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex THE LEGAL PROCESS courts statutory interpretation wex definitions courts and procedure criminal law and procedure legal education and practice statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change removes "including negligence" from the limitation of liability clause. The Perturbation Explanation notes this attempts to shield ARIN from liability even when their negligence causes damages and violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation strengthens this, saying the removal implies ARIN is not liable even for its own negligent actions. The Scraped Law Snippets don't directly mention implied duty of good faith or negligence. However, the combined explanations of the perturbation and the law state that removing negligence implies a violation of the duty of good faith, thereby creating a legal contradiction because it allows ARIN to benefit from its own negligence.
